title: Minimum Effective Dose

content.question: Where are you doing too much to hit a result you could get with less?
sop: Define what 'good enough' actually looks like for this specific result before you start
Start with the simplest version that could plausibly hit that bar
Rate the result against the target and the effort it took to get there (see calculation, page 5)
Stop the moment the result clears the bar, resist the urge to keep polishing
Note what the minimum actually was, so next time you don't over-invest from scratch
content.explainer: Effort and result don't rise together forever, past a certain point, extra effort buys smaller and smaller improvements while the cost stays constant or climbs. Minimum effective dose isn't about laziness; it's about noticing exactly where that curve bends and stopping there instead of on autopilot toward 'more.'
questions: Where are you doing too much to hit a result you could get with less?
What's something you kept polishing well past the point anyone could tell the difference?
If you had to cut your effort on your current top priority by half, what would you cut first, and would the result actually suffer?
content.equation_example: Result: 8 out of 10 (how close to the goal). Effort: 4 out of 10 (time or energy spent). Result divided by Effort = 8 divided by 4 = 2.0, maximize this ratio. A higher ratio means you got a strong result cheaply; a ratio near or below 1 means you're spending as much or more effort than the result is worth.
content.closing_statement: The last 20% of polish rarely earns its cost.

caption:
Principle Nº 215: Minimum Effective Dose

Do just enough. Effort and result don't rise together forever, past a certain point, extra effort buys smaller and smaller improvements while the cost stays constant or climbs.

Ask yourself: Where are you doing too much to hit a result you could get with less?
